description: Apollo 12 Flight Journal - Day 4, part 4: Lunar Orbit Revolutions 3 to 5

tokens: And we've had Loss Of Signal on the second revolution.
We're now 31 minutes, 21 seconds away from ignition for the LOI-2.
Scheduled to reacquire the spacecraft in about 45 minutes.
And as you heard toward the end of the of that pass , we gave the crew a go for LOI-2.
All systems on the spacecraft continuing to look absolutely nominal.
We have completed the Change Of Shift at this time.
Flight director, Pete Frank, now, taking over from flight director, Glynn Lunney.
At 87 hours, 18 minutes, this is Apollo Control, Houston.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: We're now at 87 hours, 44 minutes into the flight.
We're less than 5 minutes away from our scheduled Time Of Ignition for Lunar Orbit Insertion burn number 2.
This burn designed to lower our apolune and bring us to an orbit of 66 nautical miles by 54 nautical miles.
The burn is scheduled for some 16 or 17 seconds duration.
We're presently passing around the far side of the Moon.
We are scheduled to reacquire the spacecraft in 16 minutes.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: We should be burning LOI-2 at this time.
We're at 87 hours, 49 minutes into the flight.
We will not reacquire until 11 minutes, 45 seconds from my mark.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: This is Apollo Control at 88 hours, 1 minute into the mission of Apollo 12.
We'll leave the line open at this time.
Apollo 12 is now on it's third revolution around the Moon."
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: We have data at this time.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Delta Vcwas minus 4.4, fuel 35.4, oxidizer 35.9, increase 110.
We're in a 66.3 by 54.7 according to us.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: And we have Bat B on the charger at this time.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: You heard that report from Pete Conrad aboard the spacecraft.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: We're in the process of - Al is photographing off the target-of-opportunity chart here in order, and we're trying to get as much of that stuff done as we can.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: LOI-2 has been completed almost exactly as advertised.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Mission Control sender numbers almost precisely coincide with the onboard numbers, so we show 66.1 nautical miles by 54.3 nautical miles on the ground.
We are at 88 hours, 5 minutes into the flight at this time.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: We've gone to LM Press on our valve to bring the LM up.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: That was spacecraft commander Pete Conrad reporting that their pressurizing the LM at this time."
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: We have had no contact with Apollo 12 for the last several minutes.
However, Flight Director, Pete Frank, has gone around the room.
consulting with his Flight Control personnel and all data appears quite nominal at this time.
As Apollo 12 progresses on its third revolution around the Moon.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: If you'll give us P00 and Accept, we'll give you a clock update.
transcript.speaker: Weitz

tokens: I have a map update PAD and a landmark tracking PAD, when you're ready.
transcript.speaker: Weitz

tokens: Are you ready for your landmark tracking PAD?
transcript.speaker: Weitz

tokens: And the computer's yours, and the H-1 PAD: T-1 is 90:35:41; T-2 90:40:50; the offset is 12 miles North.
transcript.speaker: Weitz

tokens: We presently show Apollo 12 at an altitude of 63.8 nautical miles.
It's velocity now reading 5,326 feet per second.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: We presently show Apollo 12 at an altitude of 58.6 nautical miles above the Moon.
Apollo 12 will be coming into lunar night shortly.
At that time the 12 crew will routinely realign their computer platform.
We're at 88 hours, 45 minutes into the flight."
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: We're monitoring the display in Mission Control which shows the onboard data.
The crew is presently realigning their platform.
We've got some 20 minutes remaining on this pass where we'll have Apollo 12 under acquisition.
We're at 88 hours, 53 minutes into the flight.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: And if you have the DSKY or the torquing angles.
transcript.speaker: Gordon

tokens: That was Dick Gordon talking to Paul Weitz here in Mission Control Center.
We're at 88 hours, 55 minutes into the flight at this time.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: We're presently at 89 hours into the flight.
Its present altitude 55 nautical miles above the Moon.
We have 13 minutes until we lose signal with Apollo 12 passes over the back side of the Moon.
And this is Apollo Control Houston.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: We're less than 10 minutes away now from time of Loss Of Signal.
As Apollo 12 passes around the back side of the Moon Commander Pete Conrad and Al Bean are scheduled to transfer into the Lunar Module.

Flight Director Pete Frank in Mission Control just called his Flight Controllers and said if we have any words of wisdom for the crew before they transfer lets please pass them up within the next few minutes.
He's received no response from any member of his team at this time.
We're at 89 hours, 4 minutes into the flight.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: The tunnel's clear, and we're going into the LM.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: That was a call from Pete Conrad to Paul Weitz indicating that the tunnel is clear and they're ready to proceed into the Lunar Module."
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: That was Paul Weitz telling Apollo 12 we've got 5 minutes until time of Loss Of Signal as Apollo 12 passes over the backside of the Moon.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: We're 2 minutes away now from Loss Of Signal.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: One minute away now from scheduled time of Loss Of Signal.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Network has just identified to Flight Director Pete Frank that we've had Loss Of Signal.
On this front side pass very little conversation between Apollo 12 and the Houston control center.
In fact very little conversation on the Flight Director's loop as the mission has proceeded as programmed.
At 89 hours, 14 minutes, this is Apollo Control, Houston."
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: We're 2 minutes away now from reacquiring Apollo 12.
Apollo 12 now on its fourth revolution around the Moon.
As we reacquire spacecraft Commander Pete Conrad and Lunar Module Pilot Alan Bean, should be in the Lunar Module.
Meanwhile, in Mission Control Center we've had a Change Of Shift among the Capsule Communicators.
Don Lind has replaced Paul Weitz in that key position.
We're at 89 hours, 58 minutes into the flight and presently standing by for reacquisition.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: We presently show an orbit for Apollo 12 of 66 nautical miles by 54.4 nautical miles.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: You must not have picked it up.
transcript.speaker: Gordon

tokens: Pete and Al are in the LM - or Pete's in half and half, and they're going down through their checklist stuff, and I'm standing by ready to hear the P22.
transcript.speaker: Gordon

tokens: In fact - In fact, I want to go ahead [garble].You'll probably lose me on the high gain; I'll give you Omni B when I [garble].
transcript.speaker: Gordon

tokens: We just finished checking out our OPS's, and they checked out 40, both of them.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: That was Dick Gordon first responding or recently you heard from Pete Conrad reporting on their OPS system aboard the Lunar Module.
Both Conrad and Bean presently in the LM.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: We're at 90 hours, 4 minutes now into the flight.
We presently show Apollo 12 at an altitude of 65.6 nautical miles above the Moon.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: We're just standing by until time 90:30 where we do our Comm activation.
Everything looks good in the LM.
We've checked all the things we're supposed to, and they're all ship-shape, ready to go.
transcript.speaker: Bean

tokens: We're ready to go down here whenever you want to do the Comm checks.
transcript.speaker: Lind

tokens: That was Lunar Module pilot Al Bean, using the code name Intrepid, identifying that their Lunar Module ship is in fact ship shape at this time.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: We're at 90 hours, 7 minutes into the flight.
Apollo 12 presently at an altitude of 65.8 nautical miles at its point of apolune.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: This is Apollo Control, Houston, at 90 hours, 10 minutes now into the flight.

We don't expect to hear a great deal from Apollo 12 until 90 hours, 30 minutes at which time spacecraft Commander, Pete Conrad and Al Bean move inside Intrepid, will checkout the communications systems of the Lunar Module.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: We're powering up LM Comm at this time starting with our voice check on VHF A with the CSM.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: You're coming in with a lot of background right now, Pete.
transcript.speaker: Lind

tokens: Read you well except for your background, which is very high.
transcript.speaker: Lind

tokens: Yankee Clipper, Intrepid on VHF A.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: How do you read VHF A.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: That's Pete Conrad aboard Intrepid talking to Dick Gordon who is singularly manning the Yankee Clipper this time.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: How's the S-Band Downvoice Backup and Low Bit Rate?
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: We're still reading you well except for your background, which is still high.
transcript.speaker: Lind

tokens: We're going to PCM High now.
How do you pick it up?
transcript.speaker: Bean

tokens: Give us just a moment to reconfigure the site.
transcript.speaker: Lind

tokens: How's the Voice and High Bit Rate?
transcript.speaker: Bean

tokens: We're still reading you satisfactorily; again, you've got a lot of buzz in the background.
transcript.speaker: Lind

tokens: I'm talking to you on the CSM S-band, and Al's talking to you on the Intrepid S-band.
Are you sure that that's not a ground problem?
We're getting - Al's getting good reception up here on S-band with no noise.
transcript.speaker: Gordon

tokens: You're somewhat weaker, but still readable.
transcript.speaker: Lind

tokens: That's the way it should be.
transcript.speaker: Bean

tokens: How do you read now, Houston?
Down Voice Backup Biomed, Low Bit Rate.
transcript.speaker: Bean

tokens: Again, you're still somewhat weak, readable, and the background is dropping just slightly.
transcript.speaker: Lind

tokens: Read you very good on this one; your background has dropped considerably.
transcript.speaker: Lind

tokens: Houston, we just went Telemetry High .
How do you read us now, Voice?
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: Read you fine on this one, Pete.
transcript.speaker: Lind

tokens: And we are coming at you Telemetry Low and S-Band Range to Range.
And will you copy the [garble] on hot mike?
transcript.speaker: Bean

tokens: That is Al Bean and Pete Conrad interchangeably coming on the line as we are under going a voice communication check.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Intrepid, would you standby on this mode, so we can try a range acquisition?
transcript.speaker: Lind

tokens: Did you pick up our range then?
transcript.speaker: Bean

tokens: And that completes our Comm checks.
We're going to deactivate Intrepid and go back to Yankee Clipper.
transcript.speaker: Bean

tokens: That was Al Bean, reporting that they've completed their communication check, they plan to depart Intrepid shortly and return to the Yankee Clipper.
Moving somewhat ahead of the Flight Plan schedule.
We're at 90 hours, 23 minutes now into the flight and this is Apollo Control, Houston.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Would you confirm that you're in Omni Delta?
transcript.speaker: Lind

tokens: Two items we'd like to get from you before you close that out and that's the OPS storage pressure and also the GET of activation of LM power.
transcript.speaker: Lind

tokens: I gave you the OPS; they're 5800 each and let me - and let me look in the book and find out what time we went on the LM power; just a second.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: We went on LM power at 90:08:13.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: And, Houston, Intrepid just went back on CSM power at 90:29:35.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: That's Pete Conrad aboard Intrepid reporting the Ground Elapsed Times for power on and power off of the Lunar Module.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Meanwhile Yankee Clipper has started its computer program number 22 for orbital navigation.
We're at 90 hours, 31 minutes into the flight and continuing to monitor.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: We'd like you to confirm that you're going to pull the circuit breaker on the floodlights so that the light will go out when you close the refrigerator door.
transcript.speaker: Lind

tokens: The reference there by Don Lind is a floodlight on the Lunar Module which did not turn off at the time of our first transfer to the LM by the two crewmembers.
We're at 90 hours, 33 minutes now into the flight.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: We presently show Apollo 12 at an altitude of 61.4 nautical miles above the moon.
It's present velocity, 5,340 feet per second.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: The EECOM in Mission Control looking at his data has confirmed the floodlight aboard the Lunar Module is in fact turned off.
We're at 90 hours, 40 minutes into the flight and Apollo 12 presently at an altitude of 59.4 nautical miles above the moon.
This is Apollo Control of Houston.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Let me know when you've copied that.
transcript.speaker: Gordon

tokens: Let me know when you've copied the P22.
transcript.speaker: Gordon

tokens: Houston, I'm going to look at the latitude and longitude from these marks.
transcript.speaker: Gordon

tokens: Hey, Don, did you people copy the Noun 89?
transcript.speaker: Gordon

tokens: Let me know how that exercise turned out, will you please?
transcript.speaker: Gordon

tokens: We've got a state vector for you when ever you want to give us the computer, and we got a TEI maneuvering PAD P40 and a map update for you whenever you're ready.
transcript.speaker: Lind

tokens: This is Apollo Control Houston 90 hours, 48 minutes.
As you copied that last con..."
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: We'll wait for the High Gain antenna to uplink your state vector, and the rest attitude in the Flight Plan is good.
transcript.speaker: Lind

tokens: As you had copied the previous call from the Apollo 12 spacecraft, they have dispensed with using Intrepid and Yankee Clipper identifying to us that Pete Conrad and Al Bean have transferred back to the Command Module.
We're at 90 hours, 49 minutes now into the flight and this is Apollo Control Houston.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Dick, do you have any comments on the lighting conditions on H-1?
transcript.speaker: Lind

tokens: It was very easy to find the target, very easy to mark on.
I thought the lighting conditions were outstanding for that.
transcript.speaker: Gordon

tokens: Why don't you give me the map update and the TEI-11 PAD?
I'll copy them now and by that time we'll be getting over to the sleep attitude here and give you the high gain in a minute.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: The map update for Rev 5 LOS is 91 plus 11 plus 37; 180 position is 91 plus 36 plus 21; AOS is 91 plus 57 plus 45.

TEI-11 PAD, SPS plus G&N: Noun 47, 37358; Noun 48, minus 0.65, plus 0.49; GET 105:23:55.20; Noun 81, plus 3192.1, plus 0725.8, minus 0139.8; roll NA, pitch 107, yaw NA; ullage, four jets for 11 seconds; and the burn is undocked.
transcript.speaker: Lind

tokens: We have you on the high gain, and I'll go to Accept at this time, and to wrap up to you with 91:11:37; 91:36:21; 91:57:45.
The TEI-11 PAD SPS/G&N: 37358, minus 0.65, plus 0.94, plus - no - no, excuse me, it's just 105:23:55.20, plus 3192.1, plus 0725.8, minus 0139.8; NA, 107, NA; four jets, 11 seconds; undocked.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: On Noun 48, the Y-trim was plus 049.
transcript.speaker: Lind

tokens: That's what I have on the PAD.
I don't know what I read back to you.
I have 049, and how's the LM current look to you at this time?
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: We're in the process of buttoning the tunnel back up.[Long pause.]
transcript.speaker: Bean

tokens: The TEI-11 pad is a contingency pad that is stored in the onboard computer in the event a development occurred that Apollo 12 needed to return on the 11th revolution.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Would you try Omni Delta for us, please?
transcript.speaker: Lind

tokens: This is Apollo Control, Houston at 90 hours, 56 minutes now into the flight.
We will again loose signal with Apollo 12 in about 15 minutes from this time.
We presently show Apollo 12 in an altitude of 55.4 nautical miles.
It's velocity now reading 5,371 feet per second.
At 90 hours, 57 minutes continuing to monitor, this is Apollo Control, Houston.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: We did not get your state vector completed before we lost you on the pitch there, so we'll continue that now.
transcript.speaker: Lind

tokens: On this DAP load for the rest attitude, do you want me to use A-C per roll or B-D?
Will you get the readings from G&C on that please?
transcript.speaker: Gordon

tokens: We want to use B-D, Dick.
transcript.speaker: Lind

tokens: We're through setting up your state vector, and we'd like to try to get an E-dump before you go around the corner.
transcript.speaker: Lind

tokens: And we have you out our window for the first time since we've been in lunar orbit.
We've been too busy to get a look at you.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: That was Pete Conrad saying that the Apollo 12 crew is happy up there.
We're at 91 hours, 3 minutes into the flight and some 8 minutes away from that time of Lose Of Signal when Apollo 12 passes above the backside of the Moon.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: We're about four and a half minutes away from LOS at this time.
Pete Frank is talking to members of his Flight Control team to see if we have anything to say to Apollo 12 prior to Loss Of Signal.
We're at 91 hours, 7 minutes into the flight at this time.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: We're presently 2 minutes away from predicted time of Loss Of Signal and standing by.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: We're about 1 minute from LOS here.
transcript.speaker: Lind

tokens: So you can adjourn to the wardroom.
transcript.speaker: Lind

tokens: Pretty soon we're going to have movies on the fantail.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: We're at 30 seconds away from predicted time of Loss Of Signal."
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: We'll see you coming around the other side.
transcript.speaker: Lind

tokens: We've had Loss Of Signal at this time.
In that final exchange of conversation between Mission Control Center and Apollo 12, that was Pete Conrad speaking for their all Navy crew.
During this front side pass on the fourth revolution, both Pete Conrad and Al Bean transferred to the Lunar Module checked out the communication system.
They're both back in the Command Module at this time.
This was their fourth trip fourth trip during this mission into the Lunar Module.
The next time they transfer it will be for undocking and the lunar landing portion of the mission.
We're at 91 hours, 13 minutes at present.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Music: Wedding Bell Blues by the Fifth Dimension.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: We are about 1 minute away now from reacquiring Apollo 12.
Apollo 12 now on it's fifth revolution around the Moon.
The Apollo 12 crew will shortly be starting a rest period.
We're at 91 hours, 57 minutes and standing by for acquisition, this is Apollo Control, Houston."
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: We've got a couple of questions for you.
When did you activate the cryo fans?
transcript.speaker: Lind

tokens: Okay, if you will let us know, we can track the quantities a little bit better, if we know that.
Also, we are ready to terminate the battery B recharge.
transcript.speaker: Lind

tokens: Why don't we go ahead and cycle the fans for you now?
We were just - we were going to wait until just before we were going to go to sleep, but we will cycle them now for you.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: Also, Dick, the boys in the back room are very well pleased with the P22 tracking; they were impressed with the technique and the spacing.
Also the - your solution agree to within a hundredth of a degree of the map positions, so everybody is very pleased with that.
transcript.speaker: Lind

tokens: Pete, when you go in the LM tomorrow, the AGS people would like you to check a series of locations in the E-memory just to check out that system.
I've got a list of the locations and the expected parameters that we'd like to read you sometime before bedtime this evening.
They'll rest better if you have them tonight.
transcript.speaker: Lind

tokens: This is due to - what happened on launch.
Is that what you are saying?
You want to verify from memory?
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: I've got the list whenever you want to copy.
It is about a page of locations, about 25 or so.
transcript.speaker: Lind

tokens: Al's got his hands full of food bags right now.
Just a second, and I will copy them for him.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: We liked your idea of movies on the fantail so much that, while you were running around behind the Moon there this time, we replayed your last TV coverage for the boys down in the hanger deck.
transcript.speaker: Lind

tokens: It looks a lot better on the big screen than it does on my home television set.
transcript.speaker: Lind

tokens: Well, tell you what, it looks a lot better from right here with the old eyeball, but we are doing as best we can for you.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: I - I wish we could bring some TV from the back side of the Moon.
I really - I sort of am more impressed with the back side of the Moon as being prettier than I am with the front side.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: I'm not sure that that meets with complete agreement in the spacecraft, but...
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: I guess everyone has his favorite spot some place...
transcript.speaker: Lind

tokens: Okay, I'll tell you what - I'm ready...
transcript.speaker: Bean

tokens: I'm ready to copy those AGS addresses now.
transcript.speaker: Bean

tokens: Location 454, the value is plus 00700; location 466, plus 00150; location 506, plus 02400; 523 is plus all zeros; 527, plus 00500; location 537, plus 00002; location 560, plus all zeros; location 561, plus 02436; plus - or location 564, minus 46314; location 565, plus 40611; location 566, plus 01531; 601, minus 75341; location 602, plus all zeros; 622, plus 00062; location 634, plus 00100; 654, minus 62655; location 655, plus 03467; 657, plus 00015; location 661, plus 00031; location 662, plus 53603; 666, plus 04140; location 672, plus 20053; that's the end of the list.
transcript.speaker: Lind

tokens: Okay, just a second, and I will read it back to you.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: Okay, address 454, plus 00700; address 466, plus 00150; 506, plus 02400; 523, plus all zeros; 527, plus 00500; address 537, plus 00002 address 560, plus all zeros; 561, plus 02436; 564, minus 46314; address 565, plus 40611; address 566, plus 01531; 601, minus 75341; 602, plus all zeros; 622, plus 00062; 634, plus 00100; 654, minus 62655; 655, plus 03467; 657, plus 00015; 661, plus 00031; 662, plus 53603; 666, plus 04140; 672, plus 20053.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: Hey, Al, on ,our TV broadcast, when you were commenting about mountains looking like clouds, were you aware that those mountains were in the Sea of Clouds?
transcript.speaker: Lind

tokens: I guess that's where it got its name, huh?
transcript.speaker: Bean

tokens: We thought you had planned a pun for us.
transcript.speaker: Lind

tokens: No, I wasn't smart enough for that.
transcript.speaker: Bean

tokens: We've got one idea down here on the O2consumption rate that we'd like to get your comments on.

The suggestion was that, last night while you guys were asleep, the metabolic rates indicated that one of you was dreaming, probably about scuba diving, and that we think you were dreaming about petting a moray eel; and in the hyperventilation, you sucked up too much of the oxygen.
transcript.speaker: Lind

tokens: Okay, tell David, David, that I'll go along with that one.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: I suspect that it's this urine device thing - we're just out of the habit of leaving it on to keep it flushed out.
It doesn't flush out very well, so we've been leaving it on, and we usual don't bother to shut it down until the O2High Flow comes on or something; that takes awhile.
But that probably doesn't account for all of it.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: The practical people would be impressed with that.
Most of us like the moray eel theory better.
transcript.speaker: Lind

tokens: Okay, I'll go along with that.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: That's capsule communicator Don Lind talking interchangeably with Pete Conrad and Al Bean.
We're at 92 hours, 10 minutes now into the flight and Apollo 12 presently shows an altitude of 65.4 nautical miles above the moon.
It's at its point of apolune at this time.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: This is Apollo Control, Houston, at 92 hours, 15 minutes now into the flight.
The past few minutes we've had no contact with Apollo 12.
We do expect Capcom Don Lind to call them perhaps one or two more times before they bed down for the evening.
Their rest period is scheduled to start on this revolution around the moon.
Presently we show an apolune of 65.5 nautical miles and a perilune of 54.7 nautical miles for Apollo 12.
At 92 hours, 16 minutes, this is Apollo Control, Houston.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Will you have one of the doctors look at my Biomed and see if it's okay?
I had to move one of the leads.
For some reason, it reacted on my skin, and it's all welled up into a bunch of blisters here, so I had to move it.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: He reports that it - you're - they're getting a good signal.
transcript.speaker: Lind

tokens: I'm going back off the air.
transcript.speaker: Conrad

tokens: That last report from the spacecraft was Pete Conrad.
We're at 92 hours, 26 minutes into the flight at this time.
transcript.speaker: PAO
